explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **texts describing societal, ideological, or systemic conflict, oppression, or resistance** (such as laws criminalizing groups, "us vs. them" mentalities, protests against injustice, or artistic works about celebrating differences in the face of adversity).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across news/political reporting (e.g., anti-LGBT laws, police brutality, "witch hunt" rhetoric), social commentary, motivational content about "mindset" change, and even artistic descriptions involving struggle. It activates on text in multiple languages (English, Arabic, Russian, German) provided the content centers on a group being targeted, a systemic battle, or a transformative ideological clash.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: The low-activation texts are largely neutral, factual reports (stock markets, network changes, science news summaries) or announcements devoid of any framing of societal conflict, oppression, or moral opposition between groups. They lack the charged semantic field of "combat," "demonize," "targeting," "protest," or transformative "mindset" seen in high-activation examples.

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects the semantic framing of issues as a struggle between opposing sides—often with a moral dimension of injustice, resistance, or ideological transformation. It is sensitive to language that frames topics in terms of systemic battles, whether political, social, or personal.

results.plain_text: Metallica’s 10th album “will be done this summer,” according to drummer Lars Ulrich. The band will decide on a title and tracklist for the long-awaited follow-up to 2008’s Death Magnetic next month, and barring “some sort of cosmic reason,” it’ll be issued in 2016. Ulrich tells Metal Forces: “We’re now coming towards the end of the musical creative process and we’re starting to look ahead and the process of how we’re gonna share this record with the universe. “The month of June is basically when we’re gonna sit down and figure what we’re gonna do with it all – what we’re gonna call it and what’s gonna be on it.” Machine Head's Robb Flynn reunited with stolen Dimebag guitar Kiss Rocks Vegas - Cinema Review Metallica considered making Lars Ulrich frontman Ghost say covers EP will bridge the gap to new sound Metallica have been working with Death Magnetic producer Greg Fidelman at their headquarters in San Rafael, California. Ulrich adds: “Greg Fidelman is 24⁄7 on this record and has been since last summer. He’s doing an insanely great job and has been putting his heart and soul in it pretty much every waking moment of his life in helping us engineer and produce this record.” He continues: “If the record doesn’t come out this year then it won’t be because it’s not done. It will be because there’s some sort of cosmic reason that it would be smarter to hold onto it until next year. But the record will be done this summer.”

predictions.text: “If the record doesn’t come out this year then it won’t be because it’s not done. It will be because there’s some sort of cosmic reason that it would be smarter to hold onto it until next year. But the record will be done this summer.”
predictions.reasoning: It frames the album release as potentially hindered by external cosmic forces, introducing a minor conflict.

results.actual_text: One of the diseases being less treated or talked about is addiction. Society often demonizes addiction when in reality we should be treating it for what it is: an illness
results.plain_text: In the last few months, the healthcare system has–rightly so–focused entirely on the Coronavirus pandemic. Hospitals were at their maximum capacity and doctors were fighting against time to help every patient. But during this time, it seems like many diseases have been forgotten or pushed aside, something we cannot allow. Opioid Crisis is Still Here One of the diseases being less treated or talked about is addiction. Society often demonizes addiction when in reality we should be treating it for what it is: an illness, a sickness. The pandemic has not made the opioid crisis disappear, and as a country we need to multitask our efforts to fight them both. As a physician who practices addiction medicine, I can tell you most Americans personally know someone battling opioid addiction, and therefore we cannot push this disease aside. Most addicts will not ask for help due to the shame they are made to feel. As a society, we have placed a stigma on mental health issues. This is why we only see addicts instead of seeing a person struggling with an illness. Many healthcare providers have dedicated their careers to put an end to this stigmatization and provide the appropriate help. Telemedicine can Help During this pandemic, doctors like myself had to transition their practices into telemedicine. As an opioid addiction counselor, I have found this new modality can help us reach a larger number of patients suffering with addiction and other mental health issues who were too ashamed to come into the office. By having virtual access to their doctors and counselors, they can connect with us through their smartphones or computers. Everything in my career has led me to have my own addiction medicine practice to help as many people as possible. I did not want the pandemic to forbid people from getting the counseling they needed, so I expanded my practice to accept new patients in Maine and Texas to work with me via telemedicine. I hope to duplicate the same in Georgia, Iowa, Michigan, and Nevada. The main goal is to provide these people with the care they deserve. National Recovery Month The current pandemic seems to be the only health issue in everyone’s mouth, but we cannot forget about the rest. September is National Recovery Month–which not many know about–so I encourage everyone to participate in #CelebrateRecovery #MyReasonWhy and shed light on the importance of recovery. This movement encourages people to share their stories by posting a reason for hope and bring awareness to such an important matter. As we continue to fight the COVID-19 pandemic, we cannot stop helping those battling other diseases. For decades addicts have been silenced and pushed aside, it is time to change that. Telemedicine is one of the greatest tools we can now take advantage of to help those too ashamed to discuss their opioid addiction in a doctor’s office. We have the resources to right the wrongs from the past and help those struggling.

predictions.text: Society often demonizes addiction when in reality we should be treating it for what it is: an illness, a sickness. The pandemic has not made the opioid crisis disappear, and as a country we need to multitask our efforts to fight them both.
predictions.reasoning: It directly frames addiction as a societal battle with language like 'demonizes' and 'fight'.

predictions.text: As a physician who practices addiction medicine, I can tell you most Americans personally know someone battling opioid addiction, and therefore we cannot push this disease aside. Most addicts will not ask for help due to the shame they are made to feel.
predictions.reasoning: It uses the word 'battling' and describes shame imposed by society, indicating a struggle.

predictions.text: For decades addicts have been silenced and pushed aside, it is time to change that. Telemedicine is one of the greatest tools we can now take advantage of to help those too ashamed to discuss their opioid addiction in a doctor’s office.
predictions.reasoning: It describes addicts being silenced and pushed aside, framing a history of oppression and a call for change.

results.plain_text: Qué le espera hoy a Sagitario Tu impulso para hacer cosas te será muy útil hoy. Luz verde para proyectos relacionados con el campo. Amor Tu practicidad en la vida cotidiana te traerá inconvenientes en el amor. No todo es blanco o negro, trata de ser más flexible. Riqueza Tendrás demoras en trámites burocráticos debido a la dejadez y falta de profesionalismo de la persona encargada de manejarlos. Bienestar Es sólo en situaciones límites cuando ponemos a prueba nuestras capacidades ocultas. No le temas a esto, aprovéchalo para autodescubrirte. Cómo es la personalidad de Sagitario Sagitario se caracteriza por ser buscador, su cuestionamiento, aventura, espontaneidad, optimismo, filosofía, libertad, descuido. Es un signo que se identifica con el idealismo, muchas veces extravagante, jovial, amante de la libertad, exagerado y sumamente inquieto. Pertenece a un signo de fuego, de cualidad mutable. Es decir, busca encontrar una síntesis entre lo salvaje y lo humano. Su planeta es Júpiter; su casa natural, la novena y su polaridad, positiva. Es jovial y su personalidad así lo muestra, siendo sociable, siempre necesitando del estimulo de amigos, de nuevas caras, nuevos lugares, nuevos territorios, nuevos países, nuevas culturas. Es un signo regido por la curiosidad, amante de la libertad. Está dispuesto a ir más allá, pero generalmente es más espontaneo, no planifica lo que va a hacer al minuto siguiente. Es super optimista, le encuentra a todo un lado bueno. Siempre vive con la mente encendida y va atrás de nuevos desafíos. A veces puede ser un poco rudo o puede faltarle algo de tacto, ahí se le puede notar su naturaleza salvaje. También tiene una personalidad super honesta, que no tiene matices, sus actos son impulsivos, como la flecha que se dispara, va a lo alto, se manda y después si da en el blanco o no se verá mas tarde. TE PUEDE INTERESAR RECOMENDADO PARA VOS Sociedad El Gobierno anunció un recorte de 160 mil planes en Potenciar Trabajo y aumenta la tensión con los piqueteros Victoria Tolosa Paz dio detalles del acuerdo que alcanzó el Ministerio de Desarrollo Social con los piqueteros para validar la identidad de quienes reciban el plan Potenciar Trabajo. Policiales La mamá de Lucio Dupuy dio su alegato final a pocos días del veredicto: “A mi hijo lo lloro en privado” El próximo jueves 2 de febrero se conocerá el veredicto del juicio por el asesinato de Lucio Dupuy y las dos acusadas dieron hoy sus alegatos finales antes de que se sepa la decisión de los jueces. Policiales La contundente declaración de un amigo de Blas Cinalli en el juicio por el asesinato de Fernando Báez Sosa: “Estaba re mamado” En una nueva jornada del juicio por el asesinato de Fernando Báez Sosa declaró un amigo de Blas Cinalli quien explicó como era su relación y que hicieron la mañana posterior al crimen. Policiales El demoledor mensaje de Fernando Burlando a los testigos en el juicio por Fernando Báez Sosa: “Dejaron pasar la oportunidad” Durante una nueva jornada en el juicio por el asesinato de Fernando Báez Sosa, Máximo Thomsen, rompió el silencio y el abogado de la familia de la víctima Fernando Burlando se mostró muy critico al respecto. Policiales Asesinato de Fernando Báez Sosa: por qué no declarará Alejo Milanesi en el juicio Alejo Milanesi, uno de los rugbiers sobreseídos del asesinato de Fernando Báez Sosa, se retiró del Tribunal de Dolores sin declarar.

results.plain_text: BRATISLAVA, SLOVAKIA (BLOOMBERG) - The European nation that protected its population better than any other from Covid-19 at the start of the global pandemic is now trying to do one better - by testing its entire population for the virus.  Slovakia, a country of 5.5 million people on the European Union's eastern border, is preparing to launch a two-stage process using cheap antibody tests to help stop the resurgence in new cases that's sweeping across the continent.  Others have shown success in mass testing, with China canvassing cities with populations far exceeding Slovakia's and Taiwan not registering a domestic case in more than 200 days. But none has yet tried to test all of its citizens.  Encouraged by turnout of more than 90 per cent at a pilot round last weekend, Prime Minister Igor Matovic has vowed to make Slovakia the world's first country to know the full scope of contagion in its borders.  He has pushed the idea as an alternative to reimposing measures far harsher than in the spring, when lockdowns wiped out more than 10 per cent of most European economies.  "We will save hundreds of lives," he said this week.  While most testing uses so-called PCR kits aimed at identifying the virus in patients, Slovakia will rely on cheaper antibody tests in an experiment closely watched by the 27-member European Union.  This week, the bloc's executive commission pledged to spend €100 million (S$159 million) for member states to purchase the tests, which are able to provide results within 15 minutes.  The two-day programme will start on Saturday (Oct 31) at 7am and be repeated a week later.  Anyone who doesn't take part must show proof of a negative test to be able to move around in public.  In the weekend pilot, about 5,600 people, or 4 per cent, were positive and were put into quarantine.  The true rate may be as much as three times higher, however, as the test fails to identify cases in people who haven't yet developed an immune response.  Medical experts have expressed scepticism, as the authorities won't follow up positive cases with PCR tests and contact tracing, while the initiative may exhaust an already stretched healthcare system.  A day before starting, officials were still seeking enough volunteer medical personnel to administer the tests.  Critics suggest it's aimed more at shoring up Mr Matovic's flagging popularity among voters than eradicating the virus.  President Zuzana Caputova found out about the plan when Mr Matovic announced it during a news conference.  By that time, his government had already purchased 13 million testing kits for about US$5 (S$7) each.  "This isn't an expert operation, but a political operation," said Dr Marian Kollar, the head of Slovakia's Medical Chamber.  The country "shouldn't take steps that don't have any significant impact on the spread of the pandemic".

predictions.text: Critics suggest it's aimed more at shoring up Mr Matovic's flagging popularity among voters than eradicating the virus. President Zuzana Caputova found out about the plan when Mr Matovic announced it during a news conference.
predictions.reasoning: It frames the mass testing as a political maneuver rather than a public health measure, indicating a battle of motives.

predictions.text: Medical experts have expressed scepticism, as the authorities won't follow up positive cases with PCR tests and contact tracing, while the initiative may exhaust an already stretched healthcare system.
predictions.reasoning: It highlights conflict between medical experts and authorities over the effectiveness of the plan.

predictions.text: "This isn't an expert operation, but a political operation," said Dr Marian Kollar, the head of Slovakia's Medical Chamber. The country "shouldn't take steps that don't have any significant impact on the spread of the pandemic".
predictions.reasoning: It directly states the operation is political rather than expert-driven, framing a clear ideological battle.

results.actual_text: THE QUESTION Did The New York Times make Wordle harder? THE SOURCES
results.plain_text: THE QUESTION  Did The New York Times make Wordle harder?  THE SOURCES  THE ANSWER  No, The New York Times did not make Wordle harder.  WHAT WE FOUND  To understand whether the game’s gotten harder, you have to understand how the daily word is chosen.  This isn’t like the crossword, where puzzle masters decide the answers for each day. The answers were pre-determined long ago by the creator, Josh Wardle. There’s a set list of about 2,500 words that are assigned to different days.  So to see if the Times made the game harder, you can look at the source code for the original website’s word list, and compare it to the new one. (Yes, it’s possible to see the complete list, in order, so you could cheat if you really wanted. Spoiler alert: clicking those links will reveal the daily answers.)  When you download and compare the two lists, you’ll find they’re nearly identical. The only change is that the original list is six words longer.  When the Times took over, it removed six words (agora, pupal, lynch, fibre, slave, and wench) either because they might be offensive, or because they were just too obscure.  The Times did not add any new words. Any word that appears in this version of the game would have appeared in the original version too.  That means, if anything, the Times actually made the game easier.  A spokesperson for the Times confirmed this in a statement: "Since acquiring Wordle, we have not made the puzzle harder. We have not added any words to the solutions list, which was already predetermined by the game's original creator. On the contrary, we did remove a few obscure words (as an example, we removed 'AGORA'), in an effort to make the puzzle more accessible. We will continue to review the solutions, and remove obscure or potentially insensitive words."  The order of the words remains the same as the original site, too. However, since the Times removed those six words, some words were bumped up. That’s why people who still had access to the original website recently had “agora” as their answer, while most users had “aroma.” The Times did not add “aroma” to the word list; it was already next up after “agora," which was removed from the new site.

predictions.text: When the Times took over, it removed six words (agora, pupal, lynch, fibre, slave, and wench) either because they might be offensive, or because they were just too obscure.
predictions.reasoning: It mentions the removal of words like 'lynch' and 'slave' which are associated with historical oppression, hinting at societal sensitivity.

predictions.text: A spokesperson for the Times confirmed this in a statement: "Since acquiring Wordle, we have not made the puzzle harder. We have not added any words to the solutions list, which was already predetermined by the game's original creator. On the contrary, we did remove a few obscure words (as an example, we removed 'AGORA'), in an effort to make the puzzle more accessible."
predictions.reasoning: It reiterates the removal of potentially insensitive words, though framed as accessibility rather than conflict.

predictions.text: The Times did not add any new words. Any word that appears in this version of the game would have appeared in the original version too. That means, if anything, the Times actually made the game easier.
predictions.reasoning: It neutrally states the Times did not make the game harder, lacking conflict or oppression framing.
